coredata.dc:title: Development of Al–Ti–C grain refiners and study of their grain refining efficiency on Al and Al–7Si alloy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Vinod Kumar, G.S.
Murty, B.S.
Chakraborty, M.
coredata.dc:description: Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys have been successfully prepared by reaction of K2TiF6 salt and graphite powder with molten Al. While the Al–5Ti–0.8C consists of some TiAl3 particles in addition to TiC particles in the Al matrix, the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy revealed the presence of only TiC particles. The grain refining efficiency of these two master alloys has been compared with that of the conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy on Al and Al–7Si alloy at different addition levels. Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy was found to be the most efficient grain refiner for Al amongst the grain refiners studied. Even in case of Al–7Si alloy, the Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys performed better than conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. However, the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy shows poor response to grain refinement in Al–7Si alloy at higher addition levels than the Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy, indicating poisoning.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Castings
Scanning electron microscopy
X-ray diffraction
Al–Ti–C

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM photomicrograph of (a) Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy containing TiAl3 and TiC particles in Al matrix, (b) Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy containing only TiC particles in Al matrix, (c) and (d) TiC particles at higher magnification and (e) EDX spectrum of a TiC particle.
XRD patterns of Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys and those of extracted TiC particles.
Photomacrographs of Al grain refined using Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy at (a) 0.2% and (b) 1% addition levels.
Photomacrographs of Al grain refined using (a) 0.2% of Al–5Ti–1.2C, (b) 0.2% and (c) 1% of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy.
Grain size of Al grain refined using Al–5Ti–0.8C, Al–5Ti–1.2C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys at (a) 0.2%, (b) 0.5% and (c) 1.0% addition levels.
Photomacrographs of Al–7Si grain refined using Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy at (a) 0.2%, (b) 0.5% and (c) 1.0% addition levels.
Photomacrographs of Al–7Si grain refined using Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy at (a) 0.2%, (b) 0.5% and (c) 1.0% addition levels.
Photomacrographs of Al–7Si grain refined using Al–5Ti–1B master alloy at (a) 0.2%, (b) 0.5% and (c) 1.0% addition levels.
Grain size of Al–7Si grain refined using Al–5Ti–0.8C, Al–5Ti–1.2C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys at (a) 0.2%, (b) 0.5% and (c) 1.0% addition levels.
Poisoning and fading effect of Al–5Ti–0.8C, Al–5Ti–1.2C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys in Al and Al–7Si alloy. (a) Fading in Al, (b) poisoning+fading in Al–7Si alloy on long holding.
Poisoning and fading effect in Al and Al–7Si alloy

row.entry: D 2-AlSi − D 2-Al (poisoning with Si on short holding)
D 120-AlSi − D 120-Al (poisoning with Si on long holding)
D 120-AlSi − D 2-AlSi (poisoning+fading with Si on long holding)
{(D 120 − D 120S)/(D 120 − D 2)}×100 (% recovery on stirring) (settling contribution to fading)

article.ce:title: Development of Al–Ti–C grain refiners and study of their grain refining efficiency on Al and Al–7Si alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721 302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600 036, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys have been successfully prepared by reaction of K2TiF6 salt and graphite powder with molten Al. While the Al–5Ti–0.8C consists of some TiAl3 particles in addition to TiC particles in the Al matrix, the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy revealed the presence of only TiC particles. The grain refining efficiency of these two master alloys has been compared with that of the conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy on Al and Al–7Si alloy at different addition levels. Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy was found to be the most efficient grain refiner for Al amongst the grain refiners studied. Even in case of Al–7Si alloy, the Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys performed better than conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. However, the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy shows poor response to grain refinement in Al–7Si alloy at higher addition levels than the Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy, indicating poisoning.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of carbide–boride theory by Cibula [1] resulted in the development of Al–Ti–B master alloys as grain refiners for Al and its alloys. These master alloys contain TiAl3 and TiB2 particles, which act as a heterogeneous nucleation sites for Al during solidification [2,3]. Unfortunately, agglomeration of insoluble TiB2 particles has resulted in quality problems, particularly in areas like foil and electrical applications [2]. Another important draw back of Al–Ti–B master alloys is their fading and poisoning behavior in some Al alloys [2,3]. These limitations of Al–Ti–B master alloy have led to the development of Al–Ti–C master alloys [4–12]. In contrast to Al–Ti–B master alloys, very little success was achieved in promoting TiC as a potential grain refiner, primarily because of great difficulties encountered in alloying an Al–Ti melt with carbon. Another reason is the formation of Al4C3 and Ti3AlC5 under certain conditions, which decrease the grain refining potency of Al–Ti–C master alloy [4].

Al–Ti–C master alloys have been prepared so far by melting the elemental blends [5,6], reaction of carbon with Al–Ti master alloy [4,7–9], self propagating high temperature synthesis [10,11] and reaction of molten Al with K2TiF6 and graphite powder [12,13]. However, there has been no report so far on the formation of TiC alone in the matrix of Al by the melt route. All the investigations involving Al melt so far revealed the formation of TiAl3 along with TiC. Yang et al. [14], while preparing Al–TiC composites by powder metallurgy route observed that TiAl3 can be completely eliminated if the Ti:C ratio is 1:1.3.

There have also been reports that TiC is not stable in Al [15–17] and Al–Si melts [18,19]. Mohanty and Gruzleski [18] observed that TiC particles completely decompose within 30min of holding in Al–Si alloy melt. It is important to note that the existing literature on Al–Ti–C master alloys indicates that they are either inferior to [20] or as good as [4,12] conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy in terms of their grain refining efficiency. Thus, the present study attempts to synthesize Al–Ti–C master alloys that primarily contain TiC and no or insignificant amount of TiAl3 particles and compare their grain refining efficiency with conventional Al–5Ti–1B master alloy in case of Al and Al–7Si alloy.

Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C were prepared in the laboratory by the reaction of K2TiF6 (titanium source) and graphite power (carbon source) with molten Al (99.7% purity) at 1200°C for 60min. The Ti content of the master alloys was analyzed to be 4.8 and 4.9% and carbon to be 0.8 and 1.2%, respectively. The master alloys so obtained were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) microanalysis. From the bulk master alloy, TiC and TiAl3 particles has been extracted using 10% NaOH solution for XRD characterization. For grain refinement studies, Al and Al–7Si alloys have been melted (700g) in a pit type resistance furnace under a cover flux at 720°C. After degassing with hexachloroethane, the melt was grain refined with different addition levels of the master alloys. Parts of the melt were poured at regular intervals (0, 2, 5, 30, 60, 120 and 120Smin) into a cylindrical graphite mold (25mm diameter and 100mm height), surrounded by fire clay brick in order to achieve slow cooling conditions. The sample designated as 0min refers to that part of the melt, which was cast before the addition of the grain refiner. The sample designated as 120Smin refers to melt which remained in the crucible after 120min holding, stirred and poured. The grain refined samples were characterized by macroscopy after etching the samples with Poultan's reagent and grain size analysis was carried out by the linear intercept method using an image analyzer.

The photomacrographs of Al grain refined with 0.2 and 1% of Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy are shown in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. Even at lower addition level of 0.2%, the master alloy showed good grain refinement on both short and longer holding time with a conversion from coarse columnar grain structure to fine equiaxed one as shown in Fig. 3a. However, some fading is observed on long holding for 120min, which has been recovered after stirring (120Smin sample of Fig. 3a). With increasing addition level to 1% further improvement in grain refinement is visible from Fig. 3b. In addition, no significant fading was observed on long holding with 1% addition of the master alloy.

Al grain refined with 0.2% addition level of Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy exhibits fine equiaxed grain structure within 2min holding (Fig. 4 a). The grain structure remained fine equiaxed upto 120min of holding. At higher addition levels the grain structure was finer at all holding times, which could be attributed to the addition of higher amount of TiC particles. No significant fading of grain structure has been observed on longer holding. The results of Al grain refined with different addition levels of Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys are compared with the commercial Al–5Ti–1B master alloy at same addition level. Though Al–5Ti–1B is an efficient grain refiner for pure Al at 0.2% addition level (Fig. 4b), the fineness in the grain size obtained in the case of Al–5Ti–1.2C is better than Al–5Ti–1B master alloy at this addition level. Fading of grain structure on longer holding is more prominent in the case of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy at this addition level. Higher addition level of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy of about 1% (Fig. 4c) could overcome the fading effect and fine equiaxed grain structure was obtained even after long holding up to 120min.

The grain size analysis of Al grain refined with Al–5Ti–0.8C, Al–5Ti–1.2C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys are shown in Fig. 5 a–c, respectively. The grain size analysis confirmed the results of macroscopy. At all addition levels and at all holding times, the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy has shown better grain refining efficiency than the other two master alloys. The extent of fading is insignificant for this grain refiner in comparison to the other two. The results also indicate that Al–5Ti–1B master alloy has better grain refining efficiency than that of Al–5Ti–0.8C at all addition levels.

These results clearly indicate that TiC is a better nucleant for α-Al than TiB2 and TiAl3. This could be due to the fact that TiC has fcc structure, while the other two particles are non-cubic. Thus, there is tendency for larger number of facets of the TiC particles to act as nucleating sites for Al than the other two particles. The results also show that Al-5Ti-1B has a better grain refining efficiency towards Al than Al–5Ti–0.8C. This could be due to larger fraction of particles available in the former master alloy. From the stoichiometry of TiB2 it is known that 1% of B requires 2.2% of Ti for its formation and this will lead to 3.2wt.% of TiB2 in the master alloy. The remaining 2.8% of Ti in the master alloy will react with Al and results in the formation of 7.6% of TiAl3. Thus, the Al–5Ti–1B master alloy will have 10.8wt.% of nucleating particles. In contrast, in the Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy, the C will react with 3.2% of Ti (according to its stoichiometry) and will yield 4wt.% of TiC particles. The residual Ti will react with Al and will yield 4.9% of TiAl3. Thus, the Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy would consist of 8.9% of nucleating particles in comparison to the 10.8% in case of Al–5Ti–1B. Hence, the poorer grain refining efficiency of the former alloy can be attributed to its lower fraction of nucleating particles.

The present results also indicate more pronounced fading in case of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy in comparison to Al–5Ti–1.2C. The density of TiC, TiB2 and TiAl3 are 4.9, 4.2 and 3.1g/cc, respectively. Microstructural characterization has shown that TiB2 and TiC have more or less similar particle size (∼1μm), while TiAl3 is large in size (∼20μm). Based on the particle size and densities, it is expected that TiC particles settle faster than TiB2. Though the particle size of TiAl3 is larger, the studies in the present author's laboratory showed that TiAl3 particle do not settle significantly up to 120min of holding, due to their lower density. However, dissolution of TiAl3 in Al melt on long holding has been reported earlier [3], which could cause fading, at least partially, in Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. Earlier studies have indicated that TiC is more metallic than TiB2, which is more covalent in nature [21,22]. Hence it is expected that TiC has lower surface energy than TiB2. As a result, the tendency of agglomeration is expected to be less pronounced for TiC as compared to TiB2 particles. Thus the fading observed in Al–5Ti–1B master alloy could be attributed to the possible settling of TiB2 due to agglomeration and dissolution of TiAl3 on long holding.

Fig. 6 a–c shows the photomacrographs of Al–7Si alloy grain refined with 0.2, 0.5 and 1% addition of Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy, respectively. It is clear from Fig. 6a that within 2min holding after the addition of grain refiner (Al–5Ti–0.8C) fine equiaxed grain structure was obtained. Slight fading of grain structure was observed after 120min, which is recovered on stirring (120Smin sample). Increased addition level of grain refiner up to 0.5% does not result in any significant improvement in grain refinement (Fig. 6b). Further increase in the addition level of grain refiner to 1% has shown some improvement in grain refining efficiency as shown in Fig. 6c. At all addition levels, the 120Smin samples show finer grain structure. For the same addition level (0.2 and 0.5%), the grain refining efficiency of Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy (Fig. 7 a and b) is better than that of Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy. Some fading of grain structure was observed on longer holding. Interestingly, no recovery from fading has been observed in the case of Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy whereas significant recovery was observed in Al–5Ti–0.8C master alloy after stirring (120Smin sample). Further addition level of 1.0% Al–5Ti–1.2C grain refiner in Al–7Si alloy exhibits poor grain refinement (Fig. 7c) when compared to the lower addition levels, which is different from the results of Al–5Ti–0.8C (Fig. 6c).

Al–7Si grain refined using Al–5Ti–1B at 0.2% addition level (Fig. 8 a) exhibits poor grain refinement at all holding time when compared to Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys. An increase in the addition level to 0.5 and 1.0% shows an improvement in grain refinement (Fig. 8b). However slight fading is evident on longer holding even at these addition levels. Stirring after long holding has resulted in an improvement in grain refinement (120Smin) in case of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy at all addition levels, in contrast to Al–5Ti–1.2C, which has not shown any improvement on stirring after long holding. Addition of 1.0% Al–5Ti–1B master alloy shows better grain refinement than Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy at the same addition level. From the grain size analysis (Fig. 9 a–c) it is clear that the grain size obtained at 0.2 and 0.5% addition level of Al–5Ti–1.2C is finer when compared to Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys (Fig. 9a and b). An increase in grain size of Al–7Si is observed when 1% of Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy was added, while similar addition of Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys caused a further decrease in the grain size. Thus, at 1% addition level, Al–5Ti–1B master alloy resulted in the lowest grain size of Al–7Si among the three master alloys studied.

In order to understand the phenomena of fading and poisoning of Al–Ti–C master alloys the existing grain size analysis results have been used to evaluate certain key parameters, which have been shown in Table 1 and Fig. 10 a and b. The first among these is the grain size difference of Al between 120 and 2min holding. This would define the extent of fading in Al with the addition of different grain refiners. Table 1 and Fig. 10a show clearly that Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy exhibits minimum fading in Al at all addition levels when compared to Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys. However, the extent of fading in the grain size of Al decreases with increase in addition level in case of Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys. This could be attributed to the decrease in the agglomeration tendency of TiC and TiB2 particles with increasing amount of Ti dissolved in the melt.

The other parameter is the extent of poisoning with Si at short holding time. This can be expressed from the difference between the grain size of Al–Si alloy and Al after 2min of holding. Based on this parameter it is clear from Table 1 Al–5Ti–0.8C gives the best performance in comparison to the other two alloys. This indicates that Si poisons TiC and TiB2 more than TiAl3. The increase in the poisoning effect with increase in the addition level Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy could be attributed to the increased addition of TiC particles. In contrast, in case of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy the increased addition level provides more number of TiAl3 particles, which can act as potent nucleating sites for Al. Hence the extent of poisoning decreases with increase in addition level. The extent of poisoning at long holding as shown in Table 1 also follows almost similar trend as that at short holding. The extent of poisoning and fading in Al–7Si alloy grain refined with Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy is lower in comparison to the other two master alloys at lower addition level (Table 1 and Fig. 10b). However at the higher addition level of 1.0% the extent of poisoning and fading with the Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy increases and comes very close to that of other two master alloys.

Table 1 also gives the % recovery of grain size on stirring for Al and Al–7Si with different grain refiners. The % recovery as defined by {(D 120 − D 120S)/(D 120 − D 2)}×100 basically gives the contribution of settling towards fading. In case of Al, though the table shows about 100% recovery for Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy it should be remembered that the extent of fading itself is very small in this case and hence the question of recovery is meaningless. As TiC particles do not dissolve in the Al melt, the contribution of dissolution towards fading is almost zero in this case as shown in Table 1. In the case of Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys the settling contribution to fading arises from the TiC and TiB2 particles, while the dissolution is due to the TiAl3 particles.

In case of Al–7Si alloy, when Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy used as the grain refiner the % recovery on stirring is lower than the other two alloys as the extent of settling of TiC particles is lower. In contrast, in case of Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloy all the particles tend to settle down giving rise to almost 100% recovery. In presence of Si the dissolution tendency of TiAl3 particles appears to decrease and hence all the TiAl3 settle down on long holding along with TiC and TiB2 particles. The Al–5Ti–1B alloy shows a peculiar behavior in case of Al–7Si alloy in the sense that its % recovery is higher than 100%. This could be attributed to very early settling (within 2min of holding) of the TiAl3 and TiB2 particles leading to large grain size at 2min holding time in comparison to 120Smin holding. All those particles settled within 2min of holding become active on stirring after 120min of holding. In other words the number of active nucleating sites is higher in 120Smin holding sample than in 2min one. This leads to finer grain size in 120Smin sample in comparison to 2min sample in case of Al–5Ti–1B master alloy. This clearly demonstrates that TiB2 settles much faster than TiC.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloys have been prepared successfully by the reaction of molten Al with K2TiF6 and graphite, and the formation of TiAl3 could be completely suppressed in case of the later alloy.
Al–5Ti–1.2C master alloy is more efficient grain refiner for Al than Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1B master alloys both at shorter and longer holding times even at low addition levels of 0.2%.
Al–7Si alloy can be effectively grain refined using Al–5Ti–0.8C and Al–5Ti–1.2C even at 0.2% addition level. The extent of poisoning of TiC with Si appears to increase at higher addition levels.
TiB2 appears to settle much faster than TiC, which can be attributed to its greater tendency for agglomeration.
